pattern: Binary search the achievable minimum score, and check feasibility by greedily working out, index by index, how many extra back-and-forth visits are needed to reach it within the move budget.
hints: For a candidate minimum score x, each index needs to be visited at least ceil(x / points[i]) times -- work out the cheapest walk that guarantees every index that many visits.

Walking straight through the array once already gives every index one free visit; any shortfall has to be made up with extra back-and-forth steps, and a deficit at one index can often be paid for by a bounce that also helps the very next index.

The last stretch of the walk is special: if the final index already ends up with more visits than it strictly needs, you can stop the walk one step early instead of continuing on to it -- that shaves one move off the total, but only in that specific situation.

edge_inputs.description: two indices, exactly enough moves to balance them
three indices with a slightly larger move budget
move budget just barely enough to reach the last index once
move budget too small to reach every index at all, so the answer is zero
one tiny value forces many extra visits nearby to keep the minimum up
large point values (near the maximum) with a modest move budget
the weakest index sits in the middle rather than at an edge

stress.description: a long array of random point values with a large random move budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(50000, size if size >= 2 else 2))
    points = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    m = rng.randint(n, 1000000000)
    return [points, m]
